Extract the three sub-domains verbatim — III.A govern the designing and building of the model; III.B govern the collection and use of data in training and testing; III.C govern the release, monitoring and maintenance — and their performance indicators (impact assessment on the model; probability/severity harms matrix and risk-mitigation hierarchy; data quality/quantity/integrity/fit-for-purpose and lineage/provenance; unit/integration/validation/performance/security/bias/interpretability testing; model card + conformity; audits/red teaming/threat modelling; public transparency disclosures). Note: the cert page now links BoK v2.1 (effective 2 Feb 2026); the Domain III title and 21–25 range are identical to v2.0.1.

resources.title: EU AI Act — Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 (full text & high-level summary)
resources.annotation: The binding law. Extract the provider (Art 3(3)) vs deployer (Art 3(4)) definitions, the four risk tiers (unacceptable/high/limited/minimal), and how high-risk is assigned (Annex I safety component requiring third-party conformity assessment, or Annex III use case). Ground every 'the law requires' claim here; the artificialintelligenceact.eu article pages give a readable per-article view of the same text.

resources.title: EU AI Act Article 9 — Risk management system
resources.annotation: The impact assessment ON the model. Extract that it is a continuous iterative process across the entire lifecycle (Art 9(2)) with regular review; the steps (identify/analyse foreseeable risks to health/safety/fundamental rights; estimate under intended use and reasonably foreseeable misuse; adopt targeted measures; residual risk judged acceptable, Art 9(5)); testing to identify the most appropriate measures (Art 9(6)); and consideration of persons under 18 / vulnerable groups.

resources.title: EU AI Act Article 10 — Data and data governance
resources.annotation: The developer's data duty. Extract the quality bar (Art 10(3): relevant, sufficiently representative, to the best extent possible free of errors and complete for the intended purpose); the data-governance practices (Art 10(2): origin, annotation/labelling/cleaning/aggregation, assumptions, suitability, examination for biases, measures to detect/prevent/mitigate biases, data gaps); the geographical/contextual/behavioural/functional setting (Art 10(4)); and the special-category-data exception for bias detection and correction (Art 10(5)).

resources.title: EU AI Act Article 11 — Technical documentation (with Annex IV)
resources.annotation: Documentation as a conformity control. Extract that the technical documentation must be drawn up BEFORE the system is placed on the market or put into service and kept up to date (Art 11(1)); that it must demonstrate compliance with Section 2 and let authorities assess conformity; and that it must contain at minimum the elements in Annex IV. Contrast with the shareable model card and with Article 13 instructions for use.

resources.title: EU AI Act Article 12 — Record-keeping (logging)
resources.annotation: Extract that high-risk systems shall technically allow automatic recording of events (logs) over the system's lifetime (Art 12(1)), and that logging must enable identifying risk situations/substantial modifications and support post-market monitoring and operational oversight (Art 12(2)). Grounds the 'documented and auditable development/operation' point.

resources.title: EU AI Act Article 13 — Transparency and provision of information to deployers
resources.annotation: Extract that high-risk systems must be sufficiently transparent for deployers to interpret and use output appropriately, and be accompanied by instructions for use that are concise/complete/correct/clear, including provider identity, capabilities and limitations, accuracy/robustness/cybersecurity levels and metrics, foreseeable risks/misuse, human-oversight measures, and expected lifetime/maintenance. Use to distinguish instructions-for-use from the model card and the Article 11 dossier.

resources.title: EU AI Act Article 14 — Human oversight
resources.annotation: Extract that high-risk systems must be designed (incl. human-machine interface tools) so natural persons can effectively oversee them in use (Art 14(1)); oversight measures are built by the provider and/or implemented by the deployer (Art 14(3)); the human must understand capacities/limitations, remain aware of automation bias, correctly interpret output, decide not to use/override, and intervene or stop (Art 14(4)); and the two-person verification rule for certain biometric systems (Art 14(5)). Use to separate human oversight (design + use time) from human-in-the-loop (development time).

resources.title: EU AI Act Article 15 — Accuracy, robustness and cybersecurity
resources.annotation: The secure-by-design duty. Extract that high-risk systems must achieve an appropriate level of accuracy, robustness and cybersecurity consistently across the lifecycle (Art 15(1)); accuracy levels and metrics declared in the instructions for use (Art 15(3)); resilience to errors/faults and feedback loops via redundancy/fail-safe (Art 15(4)); and resilience to attacks explicitly including data poisoning, model poisoning, adversarial examples/model evasion and confidentiality attacks (Art 15(5)). This is the bridge to the AI-security mindset.

resources.title: EU AI Act Article 17 — Quality management system
resources.annotation: Extract that providers of high-risk systems must put in place a QMS documented as written policies/procedures/instructions (Art 17(1)), covering a compliance strategy, design and verification techniques, examination/test/validation before-during-after development, technical specifications, data management, the Article 9 risk system, post-market monitoring, incident reporting, communication with authorities, record-keeping, resource management and an accountability framework. Grounds 'documented development' as an organisational control.

resources.title: EU AI Act Article 43 — Conformity assessment
resources.annotation: Extract the two conformity-assessment routes for high-risk systems — internal control (Annex VI) vs assessment involving a notified body (Annex VII) — when each applies for Annex III systems, that a substantial modification triggers a new assessment (Art 43(4)), and that the outcome is the EU declaration of conformity plus CE marking. Grounds 'satisfying conformity requirements' in III.C.

resources.title: EU AI Act Article 6 — Classification rules for high-risk AI systems
resources.annotation: Extract the two high-risk routes (Art 6(1) Annex I safety component requiring third-party conformity assessment; Art 6(2) Annex III use cases) and the Article 6(3) derogation whereby an Annex III system is NOT high-risk if it does not pose a significant risk of harm (narrow procedural task; improving a prior human activity; detecting patterns without replacing human assessment; preparatory task) — with the carve-out that profiling of natural persons is ALWAYS high-risk.

resources.title: EU AI Act Article 53 — Obligations for providers of general-purpose AI models
resources.annotation: Extract the GPAI provider duties: keep technical documentation of the model (incl. training/testing and evaluation results, Annex XI); provide information to downstream integrators (Annex XII); put in place a Union copyright-law policy; and publish a sufficiently detailed summary of training content (AI Office template). Note the free/open-source relaxation of the documentation duties in 1(a)/(b) — but not the copyright policy or training summary, and not for systemic-risk models.

resources.title: EU AI Act Article 55 — Obligations for GPAI models with systemic risk
resources.annotation: Extract the additional systemic-risk duties: model evaluation including adversarial testing/red-teaming (Art 55(1)(a)); assess and mitigate systemic risks (b); document and report serious incidents to the AI Office without undue delay (c); ensure adequate cybersecurity (d). Note the Article 51 classification basis (high-impact capabilities; the 10^25 FLOP training-compute threshold).

resources.title: NIST AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF 1.0) + Playbook
resources.annotation: The voluntary US framework (released Jan 2023, sector-agnostic, non-regulatory, rights-preserving). Extract the four functions — Govern, Map, Measure, Manage — and that development-time work lives in Map (frame context/intended use/impacts/risks) and Measure (analyse/assess risk via test, evaluate, verify, validate of trustworthiness characteristics: validity/reliability, safety, security/resilience, accountability/transparency, explainability/interpretability, privacy, fairness/bias). Note the Playbook offers suggested (not mandatory) actions, and the Generative AI Profile (NIST AI 600-1, July 2024) applies it to GenAI risks.

resources.title: ISO/IEC 42001:2023 — AI management system (AIMS)
resources.annotation: The world's first AI management-system standard (published 2023). Extract that it specifies requirements to establish, implement, maintain and continually improve an AI Management System within organisations that provide or use AI-based products/services, on a Plan-Do-Check-Act basis, covering leadership, planning, support, operation, performance evaluation and continual improvement across the AI lifecycle — the certifiable, ISO-27001-style wrapper around responsible AI. Not law and not a metric library. (iso.org blocks automated fetch; facts confirmed via the ISO catalogue/OBP listing.)

resources.title: ISO/IEC 22989:2022 — AI concepts and terminology
resources.annotation: The foundational AI terminology standard (published 2022): establishes terminology and describes concepts in AI (components/functions, algorithms, data; tasks like classification/clustering/regression/generation; functions like training/validation/evaluation/inference) for use in other standards and cross-stakeholder communication. Use to anchor precise definitions the exam relies on. (iso.org blocks automated fetch; facts confirmed via the ISO catalogue listing.)

resources.title: OECD AI Principles
resources.annotation: The first intergovernmental AI standard (adopted May 2019, updated May 2024). Extract the five values-based principles — (1) inclusive growth, sustainable development and well-being; (2) human rights and democratic values, including fairness and privacy; (3) transparency and explainability; (4) robustness, security and safety; (5) accountability — as the soft-law reference that shaped the EU AI Act, NIST RMF and ISO standards.

resources.title: EU AI Act implementation timeline
resources.annotation: The phase-in dates for currency: entry into force 1 Aug 2024; prohibitions + AI-literacy 2 Feb 2025; GPAI-model obligations, governance and penalties 2 Aug 2025 (existing GPAI providers have until 2 Aug 2027); stand-alone Annex III high-risk obligations 2 December 2027; Article 6(1) / Annex I product-safety high-risk obligations 2 August 2028. Use to place when the development-time high-risk duties bite. CURRENCY CAVEAT (as of 2026-07-02): the EU AI Act timeline is in legislative flux — the Digital Omnibus on AI (provisional agreement 6–7 May 2026; publication in the Official Journal expected shortly) DEFERS the two high-risk dates from the original 2 Aug 2026 (Annex III) / 2 Aug 2027 (Annex I) to 2 Dec 2027 / 2 Aug 2028; prohibitions (2 Feb 2025) and GPAI (2 Aug 2025) are unchanged. Re-verify the current timeline and legislative status against live EUR-Lex / European Commission sources before asserting any specific phase-in date.
